data.title: Red flags at First Brands ignored by creditors
data.description:
Auto-parts maker First Brands declared bankruptcy a couple of weeks ago and left investors and creditors with billions of dollars in potential losses, yet it remains unclear what exactly went wrong.

With many investment firms eager to offer private market investment opportunities to retail clients, the failure of the large private company underscores the need for firms and investors to research closely held businesses, which, by their nature, are hesitant to open their books to outsiders. That rising demand for stakes in private companies also broadens the network of lenders and investment firms that stand to hold the bag when a private company fails.

Point Bonita Capital, a division of Jefferies' (JEF) Leucadia Asset Management that manages trade-finance assets on behalf of third-party institutional and other investors, has some $715M invested in receivables owed to First Brands. Western Alliance Bancorporation (WAL) faces exposure through a leveraged facility with a fund associated with Jefferies. Swiss lender UBS Group (UBS) was also left with potential losses from First Brands.

What's more, the U.S. Department of Justice opened an inquiry into the collapse of First Brands, as many of it finances were murky. It was reported that one of the company's largest creditors to First Brands alleged as much as $2.3B "simply vanished" amid the company's implosion. It appears the firm had used complex off-balance sheet financing and may have pledged the same receivables and inventory to multiple creditors -- a financial practice known as rehypothecation -- leaving its real collateral uncertain.

First Brands CEO Patrick James formed the company by buying local manufacturing firms through a web of holding firms. But, in 2011, a Fortress Investment Group unit filed a lawsuit, alleging he hid his control and ran undercapitalized, overlapped entities. James settled that case and an earlier fraud suit. 

Even so, his company, then Crowne Group, managed to raise $380M in 2014, as Wall Street continued to back him. From there, it rebranded to First Brands in 2020, and borrowed over $1B for more acquisitions, pushing its long-term debt to about $6B in subsequent years.

Not everyone was onboard. After learning about the prior litigation and the company's elusive CEO, several investors who explored lending decided not to engage, Bloomberg reported, citing unnamed investors. First Brands' reliance on short-term trade finance, essentially corporate payday loans backed by future shipments or inventory, often stayed off its balance sheet because they ran through James's separate financing firms, such as Carnaby Capital and Eagle Casting Holdings.

For at least two years before bankruptcy, First Brands struggled to pay vendors and deliver orders, racking up penalties, vendors, former staff and other people familiar told Bloomberg. Early this year, a James-owned affiliate failed to make $200M in monthly payments to a financing firm that had purchased some equipment from First Brands, a problem most creditors didn't see because it occurred outside the main company, court filings showed.

Trouble deepened in the summer, when Jefferies (JEF) was working up new lenders to refinance about $6B in syndicated debt, but the effort never came together before the bankruptcy.

Many investors in First Brands' first-lien loans tapped the refinancing right away, though several second-lien investors hesitated, demanding details about the company's off-balance sheet debt and questioning its choice not to hire a Big Four accounting firm, people familiar with the matter said, Bloomberg reported.

Jefferies (JEF) was said to have paused the refinancing in early August, pledging a comprehensive review of First Brands' finances by a major auditor. By early September, the company's lawyers were calling creditors to warn that First Brands was running out of cash.

Seeking Alpha editor Liz Kiesche contributed to this story.

data.title: U.S. stocks resume gains despite continued government shutdown
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages resumed their upwards run on Friday as the U.S. entered its tenth day of government shutdown, while a consumer sentiment survey came in unchanged, but better than expected.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.2% in late morning trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was last +0.2%, and the Dow (DJI) was +0.4%.

The University of Michigan's U.S. consumer sentiment survey showed sentiment barely slowing to 55.0, down from 55.1 in September, and better than the 54.1 expected.

“Current conditions did the heavy lifting as the expectation component was somewhat weaker than the consensus forecast,” said Mohamed El-Erian, chief economic adviser at Allianz.

In addition, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics has reportedly recalled some workers to get the September retail inflation report, even as the U.S. govenrment shutdown continues.

Over in the bond market, yields were lower. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 3 basis points lower at 4.11%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 2 basis points to 3.59%.

“Fed Governor Waller is on the wires stating emphatically that the U.S. labor market is the furthest thing from being tight, wage growth is cooling which means that the supply-shortage narrative is overblown, and that all this leads to no second-round inflation impact from the tariff hikes. Just in case you’re wondering why the Treasury market is rallying,” said David Rosenberg, founder and president of Rosenberg Research & Associates.

"The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield has dropped below 4.10%. WTI crude oil (CL1:COM) is trading below $60 a barrel. These developments, combined with remarks signaling future rate cuts from Federal Reserve Governor Waller (identified as one of the five finalists to replace Chair Powell), will provide a boost to stocks," El-Erian said.

Also within commodities, gold (XAUUSD:CUR) retreated from the $4,000 level, while silver (XAGUSD:CUR) has surpassed the $50 per ounce level. 

Wall Street's major indexes struggled to keep up momentum on Thursday and ended lower, as the U.S. Senate failed to pass two funding bills to end the government shutdown. Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell did not comment on the economic outlook or monetary policy at a Fed event.

data.title: Fidelity National, Global Payments stock down amid regulatory update on Issuer Solutions deal
data.description:
Fidelity National Information Services (NYSE:FIS) announced on Friday a regulatory update relating to its proposed acquisition of Issuer Solutions business from Global Payments (NYSE:GPN).

FIS shares were -0.26% Friday morning to $68.40, while GPN was 1.98% down to $85.45.

In April, FIS agreed to sell its stake in Worldpay to Global Payments for $6.6B pretax value, and to acquire GPN's Issuer Solutions business for a net purchase price of $12B.

The company is set to refile the UK Merger Notice after proactively identifying additional information to be provided to the UK Competition and Market Authority.

FIS does not expect the move to impact the previously announced projected transaction closing timeline of the first half of 2026.

data.title: Papa John's re-enters Indian market with 4 stores in Bengaluru
data.description:
Papa John’s (NASDAQ:PZZA) is reentering India to challenge market leaders Pizza Hut (YUM) and Domino’s (DPZ) after an eight-year hiatus.

The pizza chain is focusing its initial re-entry into the market with four new stores in Bengaluru to be operated by PJP Foods India and later expanding with 3 more in December and 23 next year before expanding into other cities across the country with the ultimate goal of making India Papa John’s (NASDAQ:PZZA) largest Asian market.  

“We want to prove our business model in Bengaluru first. Once it’s a well-oiled machine, we’ll expand beyond, starting with metros, which remain the low-hanging fruit, before moving into tier-two and tier-three cities,” PJP CEO Tapan Vaidja said to The Hindu BusinessLine.

With a growing middle class and urbanization of the population, the pizza industry is fast becoming a huge market in India with projections to reach $20B by 2035  -- a CAGR of 9.24% over the next 10 years and outperforming the global average.

To suit Indian preferences, Domino’s (DPZ) and Pizza Hut (YUM) feature broad vegetarian menus — an approach Papa John’s (NASDAQ:PZZA) aims to emulate with its own locally inspired offerings.

After a string of losses, Papa John's (PZZA) shares are back in the green Friday with a gain of nearly 2%.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: APLD, PATH, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged up on Friday, reversing some of the previous session's pullback after both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite retreated from their record highs, as investors weighed renewed U.S.-China trade tensions and the ongoing government shutdown.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Applied Digital (NASDAQ:APLD) +16% - Shares climbed after the company reported strong FQ1 results, with revenue soaring 84% Y/Y. The company also signed an additional 150 MW lease for its Polaris Forge 1 campus, bringing the full 400 MW of critical IT load under contract with CoreWeave (CRVW) and securing roughly $11B in prospective lease revenue over the 15-year term. Chairman and CEO Wes Cummins stated, “With hyperscalers expected to invest about $350B in AI deployment this year, we believe we are in a prime position to serve as the modern-day picks and shovels of the intelligence era.”
  • Elastic N.V. (NYSE:ESTC) +10% - Shares soared after the company raised its revenue outlook at its Analyst Day. The Netherlands-based software firm now expects Q2 FY2026 revenue of $417M–$419M (up from $415M–$417M) and full-year revenue of $1.69B–$1.7B (previously $1.68B–$1.69B). Elastic also unveiled a $500M share buyback program, with CFO Navam Welihinda saying it reflects the Board’s confidence in the company’s “business, strategy, and execution.”
  • Rocket Lab (RKLB) +9% - Shares surged after signing a contract with Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency (JAXA) for two satellite launches from its New Zealand complex, marking its ninth straight day of gains and another record high. The first Electron rocket launch, set for December, will deploy JAXA’s Rapid Innovative payload demonstration SatellitE-4, followed by a 2026 rideshare mission carrying multiple satellites, cameras, and antennas. The deal adds to more than two dozen dedicated Japanese missions booked through the decade.
  • UiPath (NYSE:PATH) +4% - Shares extended Thursday’s 18.8% surge that lifted shares to a 16-month high. The automation software maker is gaining momentum amid rising enthusiasm for agentic AI, driven by new partnerships with major players such as OpenAI, Nvidia (NVDA), Snowflake (SNOW), and Google (GOOG/GOOGL). UiPath plans to integrate its platform with Nvidia’s Nemotron models and NIM to deliver enterprise-ready AI “microservices,” enhancing natural language, image, and predictive analytics capabilities. It is also developing a ChatGPT connector with OpenAI to link enterprise workflows with its frontier models, simplifying AI agent creation and deployment.

Biggest stock losers

  • USANA Health (NYSE:USNA) -23% - Shares dipped after releasing preliminary Q3 results that fell short of expectations. The company expects net sales of about $214M, up from $200M a year earlier but below the $224.1M consensus (one analyst estimate). Adjusted EBITDA is projected at $13.8M versus $24.6M last year, while adjusted EPS is expected at -$0.15, down sharply from $0.56 a year ago.
  • Venture Global (VG) -19% - Shares slumped after losing a key arbitration case to BP (BP) over disputed LNG shipments from its Calcasieu Pass facility, exposing the company to potential multibillion-dollar damages. The International Court of Arbitration ruled that Venture Global Calcasieu Pass breached its obligations to declare commercial operations on time and act as a “Reasonable and Prudent Operator” under the sales agreement. BP is seeking more than $1B in damages plus interest and fees, with remedies to be determined in a separate hearing expected in 2026. Venture Global said it will “vigorously defend” its position and is reviewing all options following the ruling. Analysts at Schneider Capital Group noted that the market is “pricing in broader risk of further adverse rulings,” with unresolved claims possibly exceeding $5B, according to Bloomberg.
  • Levi Strauss (NYSE:LEVI) -10% - Shares plunged even after reporting better-than-expected Q3 results and raising its full-year outlook, as investors remained cautious about a “complex” macroeconomic environment and higher-than-expected import tariffs. CEO Michelle Gass highlighted the company’s strength across channels and categories, noting confidence in “sustained, profitable growth into 2026 and beyond.” Levi’s now expects FY2025 EPS between $1.27 and $1.32 (up from $1.25–$1.30), organic revenue growth of about 6% (vs. 4.5–5.5% prior), and gross margin expansion of 100 bps, while maintaining its adjusted EBIT margin forecast of 11.4–11.6%.
  • Mosaic (MOS) -10% - Shares fell after RBC Capital Markets downgraded the fertilizer maker to Sector Perform and cut its price target to $30, citing weaker-than-expected Q3 phosphate output of 1.7M tonnes caused by mechanical issues and utility disruptions. Phosphate sales of 1.6M tons also lagged production due to shipment delays. Analysts expressed concern that operational setbacks could persist for several quarters but noted potential upside if Mosaic restores production and capitalizes on elevated phosphate prices.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery gains on report about status of Paramount Skydance talks
data.description:
Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) rose 4% after a report about the status of Paramount Skydance's (NASDAQ:PSKY) efforts to purchase the entertainment giant. 

Paramount Skydance (NASDAQ:PSKY) has been in talks for weeks with Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) about a potential deal, but the sides have yet to agree on price, according to CNBC's David Faber. A potential deal would include mostly cash and some stock. 

On the deal price "it doesn't appear that a great deal of progress is being made," Faber said, citing people close to the matter. 

There are no other bidders for the entire Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) company, according to CNBC's Faber. Billionaire Oracle Chairman Larry Ellison still supports his son David Ellison, who is the CEO of Paramount Skydance, in any effort to purchase WBD. 

"Ultimately this is going to be up to the board of Warner Bros. if they hit a number that many shareholders would say, 'Hey, that's a number we can accept," Faber explained.

data.title: Dyne cut to Perform at Oppenheimer on valuation
data.description:
Shares of Dyne Therapeutics (NASDAQ:DYN) fell on Friday after Oppenheimer downgraded the neuromuscular disease company to Perform from Outperform, citing concerns over its valuation ahead of key data readouts expected this year.

Analyst Andreas Argyrides pointed to data the company presented this week at a medical event for its nucleotide-based therapy DYNE-101 in myotonic dystrophy type 1, a rare neuromuscular disease.

While data from the Phase 1/2 trial highlighted DYNE-101's effect on functional outcomes, strength, patient-reported outcomes, and overall disease burden, validating it as a potential DM1 therapy, the analyst cited concerns over the strength of data.

“We view powering of the pivotal study (mid-2026 readout) as a key overhang given the small cohort size and statistical variability,” Argyrides wrote, maintaining his $13 target on the company for the next 12–18 months.

With Dyn (NASDAQ:DYN) joining the biotech sector’s rally over the past month, the analyst moves to the sidelines, citing concerns over the stock’s valuation and a pivotal readout expected from its Duchenne candidate DYNE-251 later this year.

data.title: Consumer sentiment virtually unchanged in October, better than expected
data.description: U.S. consumer sentiment inched down to 55.0 in October's preliminary estimate, better than the 54.1 expected and slightly lower than the prior month's 55.1, according to a University of Michigan survey released Friday.

data.title: Walker & Dunlop upgraded to Buy-equivalent at Citizens with $95 price target
data.description:
Walker & Dunlop (NYSE:WD) was raised to Market Outperform from Market Perform at Citizens on improving rate outlook and favorable market conditions.

Shares were +1.36% to $81.66 during Friday morning trade. The price target was set at $95.00.

"Due to the capital-light business that is closely tied to interest rates, WD should see a boost in transaction and lending activity as a trend to even lower rates spurs more demand," said analysts Steven DeLaney and Chris Muller.

"Furthermore, futures suggest an additional 100bps of rate relief through YE26, which should put WD in a great position to take advantage of improving multifamily real estate market conditions," added the analysts.

"In addition, WD has the ability to retain earnings, in contrast to the mortgage REITs, to grow book value per share and support the further growth and scale of the company," said the analysts in the research note.

Meanwhile, sell-side analysts, Seeking Alpha authors, and the Quant Rating system see the stock as Hold.

data.title: Four out of four S&P 500 companies beat earnings estimates this week: Earnings Scorecard
data.description:
Stock index futures edged higher on Friday, following a pause in Wall Street’s rally as concerns grew over the ongoing U.S. government shutdown.

On Thursday, major indexes ended lower as momentum faded. The dip came after the U.S. Senate failed to pass two bills aimed at ending the shutdown. Adding to market uncertainty, Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell refrained from offering any insights into the economic outlook or monetary policy during a Fed event.

“Markets have struggled for momentum over the last 24 hours, as investors question how long this rally can persist, while concern grew about an extended government shutdown in the U.S. So that meant equities saw a modest pullback,” Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

“If the shutdown does persist into next week, we won’t get the CPI release on Wednesday, which will leave us increasingly uncertain about the U.S. economy… For now at least, this lack of data means we’re flying blind to some extent on the U.S. economy, and we still don’t have the September payrolls report that was meant to come out a week ago,” Allen added.

Earnings Roundup: 

Out of the four companies that reported earnings this week, all of them beat EPS estimates and surpassed revenue expectations. 

Let’s take a look at some of the companies that reported earnings this week. 

Despite ongoing economic headwinds and higher costs in its beer segment, Constellation Brands (STZ) posted better-than-feared fiscal second-quarter results, with earnings and revenue topping estimates and helping offset a 7% decline in beer sales and a lowered full-year profit outlook.

Driven by a decline in beer sales coupled with higher cost of goods sold, Constellation Brands (STZ) earned an adjusted profit of $3.63 per share, down 16% from a year ago but 22 cents better than expected. 

McCormick & Company (MKC) also topped the expectations of analysts with its FQ3 earnings report. Sales in the consumer segment increased 4% during the quarter to $973 million, including a 1% favorable impact from currency. 

The company reported gross profit margin contracted 130 basis points in FQ3. Excluding special charges, adjusted gross profit contracted 120 basis points versus the year-ago period. 

PepsiCo (PEP) beat earnings expectations and said revenue was up 2.7% year-over-year to $23.9 billion. Organic sales rose 1.3% during the quarter to miss the consensus expectation for a rise of 2.1%. 

Operating profit was down 11% during the quarter on a reported basis, driven lower by double-digit declines in the PepsiCo Beverages North America and LatAm Foods segments. Non-GAAP EPS of $2.29 topped the consensus estimate by $0.03.

Delta Airlines (DAL)’s third-quarter results, coupled with an upbeat outlook for the remainder of the year, underscored the carrier’s success in navigating a turbulent start to the year.

In contrast to the first quarter of this year, in which the carrier pulled its guidance due to the uneven economic environment, Delta (DAL) now expects to report a profit of $1.60 to $1.90 per share in the fourth quarter on a 2% to 4% increase in revenue, exceeding expectations for a profit of $1.64 per share on 1.24% revenue growth.

Earnings next week: 

There are several companies set to report earnings next week, such as Fastenal (FAST) on Monday, with big bank names such as JPMorgan (JPM), J&J (JNJ), Wells Fargo (WFC), Goldman Sachs (GS), BlackRock (BLK), and Citigroup (C) on Tuesday. 

Wednesday will include earnings reports from Bank of America (BAC), Morgan Stanley (MS), and Abbott Labs (ABT), with Charles Schwab (SCHW), Bank of NY Mellon (BK), and U.S. Bancorp (USB) reporting on Thursday. Meanwhile, American Express (AXP), and State Street (STT) will report on Friday.
